title: Complain About the Weather Like a Thai Local

vocab.explanation: ร้อน means 'hot' — it describes temperature, whether you're talking about the weather, food, or a room. In Thailand this is one of the most-used words in daily conversation. You'll hear Thai people say ร้อนมาก or ร้อนจัง constantly — it's practically a greeting. It sits right after อากาศ (weather) to say 'the weather is hot', and it works equally well describing food or a room.

exampleSentences.thai: กาแฟร้อนหนึ่งแก้วค่ะ
exampleSentences.note: ร้อน modifies กาแฟ here — same word, totally different context. Weather, food, drinks — one word covers it all.

vocab.explanation: หนาว means 'cold' — and in Thailand this word gets used very generously. Thai people say หนาว when the temperature drops to 25°C. It also describes how you feel physically: ฉันหนาว means 'I'm cold'. The rising tone on this word is important — nǎaw, going up. In Chiang Mai it genuinely gets cold in December, but in Bangkok 'cool season' rarely drops below 18°C.

exampleSentences.thai: วันนี้หนาวนะ
exampleSentences.note: นะ at the end softens it into an observation seeking agreement — very common in Thai weather small talk.

vocab.explanation: ฝน means 'rain' — this is your noun. When you want to say 'it's raining', you pair it with ตก, which means 'to fall', to get ฝนตก — literally 'rain falls'. There's no verb 'to rain' in Thai; instead rain just falls. Thailand's rainy season rain is not gentle English drizzle — it's a heavy tropical downpour, usually in the afternoon, and then it stops and the sky clears up.

exampleSentences.thai: ฝนตกมากเลยวันนี้
exampleSentences.note: ฝนตก = rain falls = it's raining. มากเลย adds strong emphasis — 'so much'.

vocab.explanation: อากาศ means 'weather' or 'climate' — and it's the gateway word that unlocks every weather sentence. The structure is always อากาศ + adjective: อากาศร้อน means 'the weather is hot', อากาศดี means 'the weather is nice', อากาศแย่ means 'the weather is bad'. No verb 'to be' is needed — Thai just puts the adjective right after the noun and you're done. Learn this one word and you can build weather sentences forever.

exampleSentences.thai: อากาศดีมากวันนี้ค่ะ
exampleSentences.english: The weather is really nice today.
exampleSentences.note: อากาศ + adjective is the core pattern. ดีมาก = very good. วันนี้ = today.

vocab.thaiSplit: หน้า ร้อน
vocab.explanation: หน้าร้อน means 'hot season' — roughly March to June. หน้า literally means 'face' or 'front' but in season names it just means 'season'. So หน้าร้อน is the hot face, the hot front — which is exactly how it feels. This is Thailand's most intense season. Temperatures can hit 40°C or above, especially in April. Songkran, the Thai New Year water festival, falls in April — and the water fights make a lot more sense when you understand หน้าร้อน.

exampleSentences.thai: หน้าร้อนนี้ร้อนมากกว่าปีที่แล้ว
exampleSentences.translit: nâa-ráawn níi ráawn mâak gwàa pii-tîi-láaew
exampleSentences.english: This hot season is much hotter than last year.
exampleSentences.note: กว่า means 'more than' and is used for all comparisons in Thai — like '-er than' in English.

vocab.thaiSplit: หน้า หนาว
vocab.explanation: หน้าหนาว means 'cool season' — November to February. This is when tourists flood into Thailand because the humidity drops and the air feels fresh. In Bangkok temperatures might reach 18 or 19°C on a cold night; in Chiang Mai December nights can drop to around 10°C. Thai people from Bangkok visiting Chiang Mai in หน้าหนาว pack like they're heading to Siberia. It's genuinely the most comfortable time of year to be in Thailand.

exampleSentences.thai: หน้าหนาวชอบไปเที่ยวเชียงใหม่
exampleSentences.english: In cool season I like to go to Chiang Mai.
exampleSentences.note: หน้าหนาว at the start sets the time context — no word for 'in' is needed. Thai puts the time word first.

vocab.explanation: หน้าฝน means 'rainy season' — roughly May to October. Now look at the three season names: หน้าร้อน, หน้าหนาว, หน้าฝน. They all follow the same pattern — หน้า plus a describing word. Once you spot that, all three become easy to remember forever. หน้าฝน doesn't mean it rains all day — usually it rains hard for an hour or two in the afternoon and then stops. But you need an umbrella with you every single day.

exampleSentences.thai: หน้าฝนต้องพกร่มไว้เสมอ
exampleSentences.translit: nâa-fǒn dtâwng phók rôm wái sà-mǒe
exampleSentences.english: In rainy season you always have to carry an umbrella.
exampleSentences.note: ต้อง means 'must' or 'have to'. เสมอ means 'always'. Both are very useful survival words.

vocab.explanation: ร้อนมาก means 'very hot' — and มาก is the Thai word for 'very' or 'a lot'. The key grammar rule here: มาก always goes AFTER the adjective, never before it. In English you say 'very hot', but in Thai you say 'hot very' — ร้อนมาก. This rule applies to every adjective: ดีมาก is 'very good', อร่อยมาก is 'very delicious', หนาวมาก is 'very cold'. Learn มาก and you've unlocked intensifiers for the whole language.

exampleSentences.thai: วันนี้ร้อนมากเลยนะ
exampleSentences.english: It's really so hot today, isn't it.
exampleSentences.note: เลย adds extra emphasis — 'so' or 'really'. นะ invites agreement. This is the complete Thai weather complaint.

blocks.speakerNote: A = visitor completely melting in the heat, dramatic and exhausted. B = Nine as Thai local, calm and unbothered. Visual cue: A wears a wide sun hat and fans herself with her hand throughout; B stands naturally with no accessories, dressed normally. Voice: A speaks louder and more exasperated; B is measured and matter-of-fact. This block is the opening clip — record it as a continuous take that can be lifted as a 30-second short.

lines.thai: ร้อนมากเลย!
lines.translit: ráawn mâak loei!
lines.english: It's so hot!
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: อากาศวันนี้ร้อนปกติค่ะ
lines.translit: aa-gàat wan-níi ráawn bpòk-gà-dtì khâ
lines.english: The weather today is normally hot.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ปกติ?! นี่คือหน้าร้อนเหรอ
lines.translit: bpòk-gà-dtì?! nîi khue nâa-ráawn rǒe
lines.english: Normal?! Is this the hot season?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ใช่ค่ะ หน้าร้อนน่ะ ร้อน
lines.translit: châi khâ nâa-ráawn nâ ráawn
lines.english: Yes. Hot season — it's hot.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: แล้วหน้าอื่นล่ะ ร้อนไหม
lines.translit: láaew nâa ùen lâ ráawn mái
lines.english: What about the other seasons? Hot?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: หน้าฝนก็ร้อนค่ะ แต่มีฝนด้วย
lines.translit: nâa-fǒn gôo ráawn khâ dtàae mii fǒn dûuai
lines.english: Rainy season is hot too — but with rain.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: หน้าหนาวล่ะ
lines.translit: nâa-nǎaw lâ
lines.english: What about cool season?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: หน้าหนาวก็ร้อนค่ะ แค่ร้อนน้อยกว่า
lines.translit: nâa-nǎaw gôo ráawn khâ khâae ráawn nóoi gwàa
lines.english: Cool season is hot too — just less hot.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ทำไมถึงอยู่ที่นี่เลยนะ
lines.translit: tham-mai thǔeng yùu thîi-nîi loei ná
lines.english: Why do I live here again?
lines.speaker: A

blocks.speakerNote: Cut to Nine in teacher mode — warm and direct. Decode the sketch briefly, then go into cultural context. Default opener uses the three-seasons pattern. Alt 1 (statistic): 'Thailand's average April temperature in Bangkok is 35 degrees — and that's the average, not the peak. The record is over 44 degrees.' Alt 2 (linguistic puzzle): 'Three seasons, one pattern — หน้า plus a word you already know. Once you see it, you can name any Thai season instantly.' Alt 3 (counterintuitive): 'The cool season in Bangkok rarely drops below 18 degrees. Thai people wrap up in puffer jackets. Visitors wear shorts. Both groups think the other is insane.'

lines.english: That sketch? She was asking about Thailand's seasons — and basically every answer was 'hot'. By the end of this video, you'll understand every word of that conversation and be able to complain about Thai weather just like a local.

Here's something most people don't know: Thailand doesn't have four seasons — it has three. หน้าร้อน, the hot season, roughly March to June. หน้าฝน, the rainy season, May to October. And หน้าหนาว, the cool season, November to February. Three seasons, and two of them are still very hot.

And when I say 'cool season' — in Bangkok we mean around 20 to 25 degrees. Thai people put on winter coats at that temperature. My students from Canada find it absolutely hilarious. But to us, that genuinely feels cold.

Today I'm going to teach you 8 words and phrases for talking about Thai weather and the three seasons — everything you need to complain, ask, and chat about the weather like a local. We'll go through each one, I'll explain what it means and how to use it, then you'll hear them all in a real conversation, and finally we'll practise together. Let's go.
Let's start with the vocabulary. I'm going to go through each word one by one. For each one, I'll say the Thai, explain what it means, and show you how to use it in a sentence. Ready? Let's go.

lines.thai: ร้อน
lines.translit: ráawn

lines.english: Our first word is ร้อน — ráawn. This means 'hot'. In Thailand this might be the most-used word in the language after สวัสดี. You'll hear it about the weather, about your food, about the sun on your skin. It goes right after อากาศ — weather — to say 'the weather is hot'. And Thai people don't just say ร้อน — they layer on emphasis: ร้อนมาก, ร้อนจัง, ร้อนมากเลย. Anything to express just how hot.

lines.thai: กาแฟร้อนหนึ่งแก้วค่ะ
lines.translit: gaa-faae ráawn nùeng gâaew khâ

lines.english: One hot coffee, please. Same word ร้อน, but now it's describing a drink. Weather, food, drinks — ร้อน works across all of them.
OK, next word.

lines.thai: หนาว
lines.translit: nǎaw

lines.english: หนาว — nǎaw — means 'cold'. Listen to that rising tone: nǎaw — going up. This word also describes how you feel physically — ฉันหนาว means 'I'm cold'. Now here's the thing about หนาว in Thailand: Thai people use this word at temperatures that would make anyone from a cold country laugh. 22 degrees in Bangkok? หนาวมาก. We're bundled up in scarves. Visitors in shorts think we've lost our minds. Both sides are convinced they're right.

lines.thai: วันนี้หนาวนะ
lines.translit: wan-níi nǎaw ná

lines.english: It's cold today, isn't it. That นะ at the end softens it into an observation seeking agreement — you'll hear this pattern constantly in Thai small talk about the weather.
This next one is essential for rainy season.

lines.thai: ฝน
lines.translit: fǒn

lines.english: ฝน — fǒn — means 'rain'. It's a noun. When you want to say 'it's raining', you pair it with ตก — which means 'to fall' — and get ฝนตก: rain falls, it's raining. There's no verb 'to rain' in Thai; instead, rain literally falls. Thailand's rainy-season rain is not gentle — it's a heavy tropical downpour, usually late afternoon, and then it stops and clears up. ฝนตก is something you'll say every single day from May to October.

lines.thai: ฝนตกมากเลยวันนี้
lines.translit: fǒn dtòk mâak loei wan-níi

lines.english: It's raining so much today. ฝนตก — it's raining, literally 'rain falls'. มากเลย — so much. วันนี้ — today.
OK, this next word unlocks everything — once you know it, you can build any weather sentence.

lines.thai: อากาศ
lines.translit: aa-gàat

lines.english: อากาศ — aa-gàat — means 'weather' or 'climate'. This is your gateway word. The pattern is simple: อากาศ plus an adjective, and you have a weather sentence. อากาศร้อน — the weather is hot. อากาศดี — the weather is nice. อากาศแย่ — the weather is bad. No verb 'to be' needed. Thai just puts the adjective right after the noun and the sentence is complete.

lines.thai: อากาศดีมากวันนี้ค่ะ
lines.translit: aa-gàat dii mâak wan-níi khâ

lines.english: The weather is really nice today. อากาศ — weather. ดีมาก — very good. วันนี้ — today. That same formula works for every weather sentence.
Now the three seasons — starting with the intense one.

lines.thai: หน้าร้อน
lines.translit: nâa-ráawn

lines.english: หน้าร้อน — nâa-ráawn — hot season. หน้า literally means 'face' or 'front', but in season names it just means 'season'. So หน้าร้อน is the hot season — and it earns that name. March to June, temperatures regularly hit 40 degrees Celsius, especially in April. If you visit Thailand in April, you have entered หน้าร้อน. Songkran, the famous Thai New Year water festival, is in April — and the water fights make a lot more sense once you know that.

lines.thai: หน้าร้อนนี้ร้อนมากกว่าปีที่แล้ว
lines.translit: nâa-ráawn níi ráawn mâak gwàa pii-tîi-láaew

lines.english: This hot season is much hotter than last year. กว่า means 'more than' — it's your comparison word, like '-er than' in English. ปีที่แล้ว means 'last year'.
Season two — and this one is everyone's favourite.

lines.thai: หน้าหนาว
lines.translit: nâa-nǎaw

lines.english: หน้าหนาว — nâa-nǎaw — cool season. November to February. This is when tourists flood in because the humidity drops and the air feels clean. In Bangkok temperatures might fall to 18 or 19 degrees on a cold night. In Chiang Mai, December nights can reach 10 degrees — genuinely cold. Thai people from Bangkok visiting Chiang Mai in หน้าหนาว pack like they're going somewhere with actual winters. It's absolutely the best time of year to be in Thailand.

lines.thai: หน้าหนาวชอบไปเที่ยวเชียงใหม่
lines.translit: nâa-nǎaw châawp bpai tîiao chiiang-mài

lines.english: In cool season I like to go to Chiang Mai. Notice หน้าหนาว at the start sets the time — no word for 'in' is needed. Thai just puts the time word first.
And the third season — bring an umbrella.

lines.thai: หน้าฝน
lines.translit: nâa-fǒn

lines.english: หน้าฝน — nâa-fǒn — rainy season. Roughly May to October. And now look at the full pattern: หน้าร้อน, หน้าหนาว, หน้าฝน — always หน้า plus the describing word. Once you see that, the three seasons are impossible to forget. หน้าฝน doesn't mean it rains all day; usually heavy rain hits in the afternoon for an hour or two, then the sky clears. The mornings are often fine. But you need an umbrella every single day without fail.

lines.thai: หน้าฝนต้องพกร่มไว้เสมอ
lines.translit: nâa-fǒn dtâwng phók rôm wái sà-mǒe

lines.english: In rainy season you always have to carry an umbrella. ต้อง means 'must'. เสมอ means 'always'. Both are great words to pocket for later.
Last word — and this one is basically a complete sentence on its own.

lines.thai: ร้อนมาก
lines.translit: ráawn mâak

lines.english: ร้อนมาก — ráawn mâak — very hot. มาก means 'very' or 'a lot', and the key rule is that it always goes AFTER the adjective in Thai, never before it. Not มากร้อน — that's wrong. ร้อนมาก — hot, then very. This applies to everything: ดีมาก is very good, อร่อยมาก is very delicious, หนาวมาก is very cold. Learn มาก and you've unlocked intensifiers for the whole language in one go.

lines.thai: วันนี้ร้อนมากเลยนะ
lines.translit: wan-níi ráawn mâak loei ná

lines.english: It's really so hot today, isn't it. เลย adds extra punch — 'so' or 'really'. นะ invites the other person to agree. This is the complete Thai weather complaint, and you will use it every single day.

OK, so now you know all eight words and phrases. What I'm going to do now is put them together into a short conversation — imagine two Thai friends meeting on a hot afternoon and doing what Thai people do: complain about the weather. I'm going to speak at natural speed, the way a Thai person would actually talk. Don't pause the video. Just listen and see how much you can catch. Ready? Here we go.

lines.thai: อากาศวันนี้ร้อนมากเลยนะ
ใช่เลย หน้าร้อนนี้ร้อนกว่าปีที่แล้ว
หน้าหนาวที่นี่ไม่ค่อยหนาวเลย
แต่หน้าฝนฝนตกทุกวันเลยนะ
ชอบหน้าไหนมากที่สุดคะ
ชอบหน้าหนาวค่ะ อากาศดีกว่า

lines.english: How was that? Could you catch some of those words? Don't worry if you didn't get everything — that's completely normal at this stage. Now let's go through it sentence by sentence and I'll show you exactly what each part means.
I'm going to go through that same conversation again, but this time sentence by sentence. I'll say the Thai, and then I'll explain exactly what each part means.

lines.thai: อากาศวันนี้ร้อนมากเลยนะ
lines.translit: aa-gàat wan-níi ráawn mâak loei ná
aa-gàat wan-níi ráawn mâak loei ná

lines.english: The weather today is so hot, isn't it.

อากาศ — weather. วันนี้ — today. ร้อนมาก — very hot. เลย adds extra emphasis, like 'so' or 'really'. And นะ at the very end is a particle that softens the statement and invites the listener to agree — 'isn't it?' Notice there's no verb 'is' anywhere in this sentence. อากาศ goes straight into the adjective ร้อน with nothing in between. That's Thai sentence structure: noun, then adjective, done.

lines.thai: ใช่เลย หน้าร้อนนี้ร้อนกว่าปีที่แล้ว
lines.translit: châi loei nâa-ráawn níi ráawn gwàa pii-tîi-láaew
châi loei nâa-ráawn níi ráawn gwàa pii-tîi-láaew

lines.english: Right! This hot season is hotter than last year.

ใช่เลย — 'exactly' or 'that's right', with เลย adding emphasis. หน้าร้อนนี้ — this hot season, where นี้ means 'this'. Then ร้อนกว่า — 'hotter than'. กว่า is your comparison word: adjective plus กว่า means '-er than'. ปีที่แล้ว — last year. So the comparison formula is: adjective + กว่า + what you're comparing to. Simple, and it works for every comparison in Thai.

lines.thai: หน้าหนาวที่นี่ไม่ค่อยหนาวเลย
lines.translit: nâa-nǎaw thîi-nîi mâi khôi nǎaw loei
nâa-nǎaw thîi-nîi mâi khôi nǎaw loei

lines.english: The cool season here isn't really cold at all.

หน้าหนาว — cool season. ที่นี่ — here. Then ไม่ค่อย — this is a really useful phrase meaning 'not very' or 'not really'. It's softer than a flat ไม่ (not). And หนาวเลย — cold at all. So the full meaning: cool season here, not really cold at all. ไม่ค่อย is one of those phrases that makes your Thai sound genuinely natural — use it whenever something 'isn't really' the way you'd expect.

lines.thai: แต่หน้าฝนฝนตกทุกวันเลยนะ
lines.translit: dtàae nâa-fǒn fǒn dtòk thúk wan loei ná
dtàae nâa-fǒn fǒn dtòk thúk wan loei ná

lines.english: But in the rainy season it rains every single day.

แต่ — 'but'. หน้าฝน — rainy season. Then ฝนตก — 'it rains', literally 'rain falls'. ทุกวัน — every day. เลยนะ at the end emphasises and seeks agreement. Notice ฝน appears twice in this sentence: once in หน้าฝน as part of the season name, and once in ฝนตก as the action. Thai doesn't find that repetitive at all — it's completely natural.

lines.thai: ชอบหน้าไหนมากที่สุดคะ
lines.translit: châawp nâa nǎi mâak thîi-sùt khá
châawp nâa nǎi mâak thîi-sùt khá

lines.english: Which season do you like the most?

ชอบ means 'to like'. หน้าไหน — which season — ไหน is the question word 'which'. มากที่สุด — the most, literally 'a lot, the utmost'. คะ is the polite question particle for women. Notice ไหน sits in the middle of the phrase — 'like which season' — rather than jumping to the front the way English question words do. In Thai, question words tend to stay in position. That's a pattern worth remembering.

lines.thai: ชอบหน้าหนาวค่ะ อากาศดีกว่า
lines.translit: châawp nâa-nǎaw khâ aa-gàat dii gwàa
châawp nâa-nǎaw khâ aa-gàat dii gwàa

lines.english: I like cool season — the weather is nicer.

ชอบหน้าหนาว — I like cool season. ค่ะ makes it a polite statement. Then อากาศดีกว่า — 'the weather is better', literally 'weather good more-than'. กว่า at the end without naming what it's compared to just implies 'than the others' — the other seasons are obvious from context. Short, natural, and very Thai.

Now it's your turn to practise. I'm going to say each sentence slowly, and I want you to repeat after me. Don't be shy — say it out loud! Speaking is how you learn. I'll leave a gap after each sentence for you to repeat.

lines.thai: อากาศวันนี้ร้อนมากเลยนะ
lines.thaiSplit: อากาศ วันนี้ ร้อน มาก เลย นะ
lines.translit: aa-gàat wan-níi ráawn mâak loei ná

lines.thai: ใช่เลย หน้าร้อนนี้ร้อนกว่าปีที่แล้ว
lines.thaiSplit: ใช่ เลย หน้าร้อน นี้ ร้อน กว่า ปี ที่แล้ว
lines.translit: châi loei nâa-ráawn níi ráawn gwàa pii-tîi-láaew

lines.thai: หน้าหนาวที่นี่ไม่ค่อยหนาวเลย
lines.thaiSplit: หน้าหนาว ที่นี่ ไม่ค่อย หนาว เลย
lines.translit: nâa-nǎaw thîi-nîi mâi khôi nǎaw loei

lines.thai: แต่หน้าฝนฝนตกทุกวันเลยนะ
lines.thaiSplit: แต่ หน้าฝน ฝน ตก ทุกวัน เลย นะ
lines.translit: dtàae nâa-fǒn fǒn dtòk thúk wan loei ná

lines.thai: ชอบหน้าไหนมากที่สุดคะ
lines.thaiSplit: ชอบ หน้า ไหน มาก ที่สุด คะ
lines.translit: châawp nâa nǎi mâak thîi-sùt khá

lines.thai: ชอบหน้าหนาวค่ะ อากาศดีกว่า
lines.thaiSplit: ชอบ หน้าหนาว ค่ะ อากาศ ดี กว่า
lines.translit: châawp nâa-nǎaw khâ aa-gàat dii gwàa

lines.english: Great job! If that felt difficult, that's completely fine — rewind and do it again. Repetition is how you get better. The more times you say these sentences out loud, the more natural they start to feel.
OK, quiz time! I'm going to give you a situation in English, and you need to say the Thai phrase. I'll give you a few seconds to think, and then I'll give you the answer. Ready?

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Ask the question, then hold 3 seconds of silence.

lines.english: You're chatting with a Thai friend and want to say: 'The weather today is very hot.' How do you say it in Thai?
Try saying it now...

blocks.speakerNote: Smile warmly. Say it clearly at natural speed.

lines.thai: อากาศวันนี้ร้อนมาก
lines.translit: aa-gàat wan-níi ráawn mâak

lines.english: That's right — อากาศวันนี้ร้อนมาก means 'the weather today is very hot.' อากาศ is weather, วันนี้ is today, ร้อนมาก is very hot. No verb needed — Thai goes straight from noun to adjective.

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Pause 3 seconds after asking.

lines.english: Someone asks which season you like the most. You love cool season. How do you answer in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ชอบหน้าหนาวค่ะ
lines.translit: châawp nâa-nǎaw khâ

lines.english: That's right — ชอบหน้าหนาวค่ะ means 'I like cool season.' ชอบ is 'to like', หน้าหนาว is 'cool season', and ค่ะ makes it polite. Short and natural.

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Pause 3 seconds after asking.

lines.english: You're warning a friend visiting Thailand: in rainy season it rains every day. How do you say that in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: หน้าฝนฝนตกทุกวัน
lines.translit: nâa-fǒn fǒn dtòk thúk wan

lines.english: That's right — หน้าฝนฝนตกทุกวัน means 'in rainy season it rains every day.' หน้าฝน sets the season, ฝนตก means it's raining, ทุกวัน means every day. Simple and clear.
Amazing work. Let's bring it all together with a quick recap.
Let's do a quick recap of everything you learned today.

You now know Thailand's three seasons, how to describe the weather, and how to complain about the heat like a local — which, honestly, is a national pastime here. Thanks for watching! If this helped you, give it a thumbs up and subscribe so you don't miss the next one.

blocks.speakerNote: Pre-Rev-6 — may need retro-fit when template updates. Tease next topic warmly, hold up the phrase clearly.

lines.english: Next time, we're heading to the market — because every Thai person's second favourite thing after complaining about the weather is shopping. I'll teach you how to ask for prices, haggle, and buy things in Thai. Here's your preview phrase:

lines.thai: ราคาเท่าไหร่คะ
lines.translit: raa-khaa thâo-rài khá
